documents.title: AWS Compute Service Selection
documents.summary: Choose AWS compute by the level of control, packaging model, and operational ownership you want to keep.
key_points: EC2 fits workloads that need VM-level control and custom operating-system behavior.
Lambda fits event-driven code when you want to avoid managing servers.
ECS, EKS, and Fargate fit containerized workloads with different tradeoffs in control and complexity.
examples: Use App Runner or Elastic Beanstalk when you want a simpler deployment experience than raw infrastructure.
Use Batch when the workload is queued job processing rather than request-response APIs.
pitfalls: Choosing the most flexible option by default and inheriting avoidable operational burden.
Treating every compute choice as interchangeable when workload shape is different.
avoid_when: Avoid premature standardization on one compute service before understanding runtime, scaling, and operations needs.

documents.title: Amazon EC2
documents.summary: EC2 provides resizable virtual machines when you need operating-system control, custom runtimes, or compatibility with server-based software.
key_points: EC2 is flexible but pushes more patching, scaling, and instance hygiene onto your team.
It is useful for lift-and-shift migrations and workloads with host-level dependencies.
Production use usually still depends on Auto Scaling, load balancing, and monitoring.
examples: Run legacy applications that expect a server and local disk semantics.
Host software with custom agents or kernel-level requirements.
pitfalls: Using EC2 by default when a managed platform would reduce operational load.
Ignoring idle capacity cost and scaling lag.
avoid_when: Avoid EC2 when a serverless or managed container option already fits the workload well.

documents.title: AWS Lambda
documents.summary: Lambda is event-driven serverless compute for bursty, asynchronous, or lightweight request-driven workloads.
key_points: Lambda is strongest when execution is short-lived and naturally triggered by events.
It reduces server management but not application design complexity.
Concurrency and downstream system limits still matter in serverless architectures.
examples: React to S3 uploads, queue messages, or scheduled events.
Run lightweight APIs behind API Gateway.
pitfalls: Using Lambda for long-running, stateful, or connection-heavy workloads.
Ignoring cold starts or concurrency spikes in latency-sensitive paths.
avoid_when: Avoid Lambda when workloads need long execution windows, stable in-memory state, or specialized runtime control.

documents.title: AWS Fargate
documents.summary: Fargate provides serverless compute for containers so teams can use container packaging without managing worker nodes.
key_points: Fargate is used with ECS or EKS rather than as a standalone orchestration layer.
It reduces cluster operations compared with self-managed container hosts.
It is best when container boundaries matter but infrastructure management should stay small.
examples: Run microservices packaged as containers without managing EC2-backed clusters.
Operate internal APIs that need container control but not host administration.
pitfalls: Choosing Fargate without validating steady-state cost at high scale.
Assuming serverless containers remove all performance tuning concerns.
avoid_when: Avoid Fargate when low-level host control or specialized node configuration is a hard requirement.

documents.title: Amazon ECS
documents.summary: ECS is AWS-native container orchestration for teams that want managed container scheduling with less control-plane complexity than Kubernetes.
key_points: ECS fits teams that want deep AWS integration and simpler operations.
It can run on EC2 or Fargate depending on infrastructure preferences.
It is a strong choice when Kubernetes portability is not a hard requirement.
examples: Run internal microservices with IAM, load balancing, and AWS-native deployment flows.
Standardize on containers without adopting Kubernetes complexity.
pitfalls: Choosing ECS when the organization has a non-negotiable Kubernetes platform standard.
Treating orchestration as solved without defining deployment, observability, and scaling patterns.
avoid_when: Avoid ECS when Kubernetes APIs and ecosystem compatibility are a core requirement.

documents.title: Amazon EKS
documents.summary: EKS is managed Kubernetes on AWS for teams that need Kubernetes APIs, tooling, and ecosystem compatibility.
key_points: EKS preserves the Kubernetes operating model while AWS manages part of the control plane.
It is useful when teams already invest in Kubernetes skills or tooling.
It usually brings more platform complexity than ECS.
examples: Run workloads that depend on Kubernetes-native deployment patterns or operators.
Align with an existing multi-team Kubernetes platform strategy.
pitfalls: Choosing EKS for prestige instead of real platform needs.
Underestimating cluster operations, networking, and upgrade governance.
avoid_when: Avoid EKS when simpler AWS-native orchestration is sufficient.

documents.title: AWS Analytics Service Selection
documents.summary: Choose AWS analytics services by whether the need is ad hoc SQL, warehousing, streaming, or distributed data processing.
key_points: Athena is best for querying data in S3 without standing up query infrastructure.
Redshift fits governed warehouse-style analytics with stronger performance expectations.
EMR and Flink fit managed big-data and stream-processing needs when teams need more control.
examples: Use Firehose for managed delivery and Kinesis Data Streams when custom consumers are required.
Use OpenSearch for search and log exploration rather than warehouse-style analytics.
pitfalls: Treating every analytics workload as the same just because the input data is in S3.
Ignoring whether the team needs warehouse, streaming, or ETL behavior.
avoid_when: Avoid selecting analytics services by familiarity alone; start from query pattern, latency, and governance needs.

documents.title: Amazon Athena
documents.summary: Athena is serverless SQL over data in S3 for ad hoc analysis and lightweight reporting.
key_points: Athena removes the need to provision query infrastructure for many lake queries.
It is well suited to exploratory analysis and lightweight governed reporting.
It is not the same as a tuned warehouse for heavy sustained workloads.
examples: Query raw or curated data in S3 for investigations and quick reports.
Support analysts who need SQL access without standing up clusters.
pitfalls: Using Athena for sustained warehouse workloads that need tight performance control.
Ignoring data layout and partition strategy in S3.
avoid_when: Avoid Athena when concurrency, modeled warehouse workloads, or predictable performance dominate.

documents.title: Amazon Redshift
documents.summary: Redshift is a managed data warehouse for analytical workloads that need stronger performance and warehouse-style governance than ad hoc lake queries.
key_points: Redshift fits sustained analytics, curated datasets, and higher-query concurrency.
It is stronger than ad hoc query services when a warehouse model is justified.
It still requires thoughtful modeling, workload planning, and cost discipline.
examples: Support business intelligence dashboards over curated datasets.
Run recurring analytical queries with tighter performance expectations.
pitfalls: Using a warehouse before the data model and reporting needs are mature enough.
Assuming a managed warehouse removes the need for modeling and tuning.
avoid_when: Avoid Redshift when the workload is mostly exploratory SQL over raw S3 data.

documents.title: Amazon EMR
documents.summary: EMR provides managed big-data infrastructure for frameworks such as Spark and Hadoop when teams need large-scale distributed processing control.
key_points: EMR is stronger than lightweight serverless analytics tools when custom distributed processing matters.
It gives more control over framework-driven data processing environments.
That flexibility comes with more operational and cost-management responsibility.
examples: Run large-scale Spark transformations and batch processing pipelines.
Process datasets too complex for lightweight query-only services.
pitfalls: Choosing EMR when the real need is just ad hoc SQL or simple ETL.
Underestimating job orchestration and cluster cost management.
avoid_when: Avoid EMR when serverless query or lighter ETL services already fit the problem.

documents.title: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
documents.summary: Kinesis Data Streams is for durable, low-latency streaming ingestion when applications need custom consumers over event streams.
key_points: It supports real-time stream consumption patterns rather than just managed landing into destinations.
It is useful when teams need control over consumers and stream-processing behavior.
It is part of a broader streaming architecture, not the whole design.
examples: Feed stream processors or custom consumer applications in near real time.
Capture application or device events for downstream analytics.
pitfalls: Using Data Streams when the workload only needs managed delivery into storage.
Ignoring consumer scaling and downstream pressure.
avoid_when: Avoid Kinesis Data Streams when simple delivery is enough and no custom consumption logic is needed.

documents.title: Amazon Data Firehose
documents.summary: Data Firehose is for managed streaming delivery into destinations such as S3 and Redshift with less operational work than custom stream consumption.
key_points: Firehose is oriented around reliable landing and delivery rather than general-purpose streaming consumers.
It is the simpler choice when the destination matters more than custom stream processing.
It reduces stream-management burden compared with building custom consumers.
examples: Land application or telemetry data in S3 for later analytics.
Deliver streaming data into analytics destinations with minimal operational code.
pitfalls: Choosing Firehose when the application really needs custom near-real-time consumers.
Confusing delivery convenience with full stream-processing capability.
avoid_when: Avoid Firehose when custom event consumers and fine-grained stream control are core requirements.

documents.title: AWS Storage Service Selection
documents.summary: Choose AWS storage by access semantics first: objects, block devices, or shared filesystems are different architectural tools.
key_points: S3 is object storage for durability, scale, and large datasets or assets.
EBS is block storage for compute-attached volumes and transactional disk patterns.
EFS is shared file storage for multiple compute resources that need a common filesystem.
examples: Use S3 for backups and static assets, EBS for databases on instances, and EFS for shared content workflows.
Storage mistakes usually come from forcing one access pattern into another.
pitfalls: Using object storage like a mounted disk.
Choosing shared file storage when the application really wants object semantics.
avoid_when: Avoid selecting storage by habit; start from access pattern, sharing needs, and durability model.

documents.title: Amazon S3
documents.summary: S3 is durable object storage for files, logs, backups, static assets, and lake-style datasets.
key_points: S3 is built around object semantics rather than mounted block or POSIX file behavior.
It is foundational for backups, static distribution, and data lake patterns.
Lifecycle, encryption, and access control are central design features.
examples: Store application documents and media files.
Use S3 as a raw and curated analytics storage layer.
pitfalls: Treating S3 like a low-latency transactional database.
Ignoring object naming and access-policy design.
avoid_when: Avoid S3 when the workload needs attached disk semantics or shared POSIX behavior.

documents.title: Amazon EBS
documents.summary: EBS is persistent block storage intended for use with compute instances that need attached disks.
key_points: EBS fits boot volumes, transactional workloads, and software that expects block devices.
It is tied to compute-centric storage patterns rather than global object access.
It is not a shared multi-writer filesystem substitute.
examples: Provide volumes for EC2-hosted applications and databases.
Use attached storage for instance-based transactional systems.
pitfalls: Using EBS for multi-instance shared file access.
Treating block storage as if it solved durability and operations by itself.
avoid_when: Avoid EBS when the real need is object storage or a shared filesystem.

documents.title: Amazon EFS
documents.summary: EFS is managed shared file storage for workloads that need a common filesystem across multiple compute resources.
key_points: EFS is useful when many services or instances need shared file access.
It fits file-oriented patterns better than object storage or attached block devices.
It solves sharing semantics, not every performance or cost concern.
examples: Share content or application assets across multiple compute nodes.
Support workloads that expect a network file system.
pitfalls: Using EFS when the application would be simpler with object storage.
Choosing shared storage without validating actual access patterns.
avoid_when: Avoid EFS when the workload is object-centric or needs only single-instance attached storage.

documents.title: AWS Database Service Selection
documents.summary: Choose AWS databases from the data model and access pattern, not from habit or brand familiarity.
key_points: Relational engines such as RDS and Aurora fit transactional SQL workloads.
DynamoDB fits large-scale low-latency key-value and document access patterns.
Specialized services such as Neptune and Timestream are better when graph or time-series behavior dominates.
examples: Start with access pattern, consistency needs, and scale shape before selecting the engine.
Specialized data models often outperform forced general-purpose compromises.
pitfalls: Moving relational schemas directly into non-relational databases.
Using one database family for every workload to reduce cognitive load.
avoid_when: Avoid database choices driven by team familiarity alone when the workload shape clearly points elsewhere.

documents.title: Amazon Aurora
documents.summary: Aurora is a managed relational database service designed for higher-performance, cloud-native relational workloads with MySQL or PostgreSQL compatibility.
key_points: Aurora targets demanding relational workloads that still want familiar SQL engines.
It is often stronger than standard RDS engines when performance and resilience expectations are higher.
It remains a relational system, so schema and query design still matter.
examples: Back transaction-heavy applications that need managed SQL with stronger cloud-native capabilities.
Use when relational consistency matters but self-managed tuning is undesirable.
pitfalls: Choosing Aurora just because it is the premium relational option.
Ignoring whether the workload actually needs relational semantics at all.
avoid_when: Avoid Aurora when the workload is better modeled as key-value, document, graph, or time-series data.

documents.title: Amazon RDS
documents.summary: RDS is managed relational database infrastructure for workloads that benefit from SQL, joins, and transactional correctness.
key_points: RDS reduces operational burden compared with self-managing database servers.
It is a strong fit for line-of-business systems and common transactional applications.
Read scaling and performance still require architectural decisions.
examples: Support transactional business applications that rely on SQL and relational consistency.
Run traditional OLTP-style systems without self-managing the full database stack.
pitfalls: Assuming managed means no tuning or schema discipline.
Using read replicas for stale-sensitive workloads without acknowledging lag.
avoid_when: Avoid RDS when the workload clearly needs large-scale non-relational access patterns.

documents.title: Amazon DynamoDB
documents.summary: DynamoDB is a managed key-value and document database for high-scale, low-latency access when access patterns are known upfront.
key_points: Data modeling starts from access patterns and partition behavior, not normalized relational entities.
DynamoDB is built for predictable millisecond access at scale.
Good key design is the difference between success and hot-partition failure.
examples: Store user sessions, carts, or application metadata at high scale.
Support event-driven systems that need fast, managed state access.
pitfalls: Porting a relational schema directly into DynamoDB.
Ignoring partition-key distribution until production traffic exposes it.
avoid_when: Avoid DynamoDB when ad hoc joins, flexible relational querying, or warehouse-style SQL dominate.

documents.title: Amazon Neptune
documents.summary: Neptune is a graph database for workloads where relationships and traversals are the primary query model.
key_points: Graph databases shine when linked entities and multi-hop relationships are central.
Neptune is a better fit than relational or document models for relationship-heavy queries.
It is specialized and should be chosen only when graph behavior is truly core.
examples: Model fraud link analysis, knowledge graphs, or recommendation relationships.
Support applications where traversing connections matters more than row-based transactions.
pitfalls: Using a graph database for workloads that are really simple key-value or relational transactions.
Choosing a specialized engine before validating the query model.
avoid_when: Avoid Neptune when relationships are not central to the access pattern.

documents.title: Amazon Timestream
documents.summary: Timestream is a purpose-built time-series database for metrics, telemetry, and timestamp-driven event data.
key_points: Timestream fits workloads where time windows, recency, and retention policies are central.
It is better than general-purpose databases when time-series query patterns dominate.
It is optimized for metrics and telemetry style access rather than generic transactions.
examples: Store operational metrics, IoT readings, or timestamped telemetry.
Query recent and historical trends across time windows.
pitfalls: Using a specialized time-series service for general application state.
Ignoring retention and aggregation strategy.
avoid_when: Avoid Timestream when the workload is not fundamentally time-series in nature.

documents.title: AWS Networking and Edge Selection
documents.summary: AWS networking choices shape isolation, traffic steering, and global delivery before application logic even runs.
key_points: VPC is the foundational network boundary for most AWS architectures.
Route 53 handles DNS and traffic steering, not just domain resolution.
CloudFront improves global delivery and edge protection when users are geographically distributed.
examples: Use Transit Gateway when many VPCs and on-premises networks need manageable connectivity.
Treat network design as a core architectural decision rather than infrastructure afterthought.
pitfalls: Delaying network design until after service choices are made.
Assuming DNS, CDN, and network isolation are interchangeable concerns.
avoid_when: Avoid oversimplifying networking in multi-account, hybrid, or global systems.

documents.title: Amazon VPC
documents.summary: VPC provides the isolated networking foundation for AWS resources, including subnets, routing, and traffic control boundaries.
key_points: VPC design influences security, connectivity, and availability from the start.
Subnet and routing structure affect how services communicate and how exposure is controlled.
Good VPC design supports later scaling and governance rather than constraining it.
examples: Segment public-facing and internal workloads into different subnet patterns.
Establish network boundaries for multi-tier applications.
pitfalls: Treating networking as a default setup rather than a deliberate design choice.
Creating overly complex network topology without clear segmentation goals.
avoid_when: Avoid ad hoc VPC growth without clear routing and isolation standards.

documents.title: Amazon CloudFront
documents.summary: CloudFront is a content delivery network for low-latency global delivery of static content, APIs, downloads, and media.
key_points: CloudFront reduces latency for distributed users by serving content closer to them.
It can also improve origin protection and integrate with edge security controls.
It is valuable even for APIs when edge performance and protection matter.
examples: Serve static assets and downloads globally.
Put CloudFront in front of web apps or APIs for edge caching and protection.
pitfalls: Adding a CDN without understanding caching behavior and invalidation needs.
Assuming CloudFront replaces network design or DNS strategy.
avoid_when: Avoid CloudFront when the workload gets no value from edge delivery or caching.

documents.title: Amazon Route 53
documents.summary: Route 53 combines DNS with health checks and routing policies that help shape resilient traffic flow.
key_points: Route 53 is more than domain hosting because routing policies affect resilience and latency outcomes.
It supports failover, weighted, and latency-aware routing patterns.
It is a control point for how users reach services globally.
examples: Use failover routing to direct traffic away from unhealthy endpoints.
Steer users based on latency or weighted rollout strategies.
pitfalls: Thinking of DNS as static configuration rather than part of resilience design.
Ignoring health-check and failover behavior in recovery planning.
avoid_when: Avoid simplistic DNS setup when the system needs explicit global traffic policies.

documents.title: AWS Identity and Access Management
documents.summary: IAM is the core AWS authorization system for identities, roles, and least-privilege access control.
key_points: IAM determines who can do what across AWS services.
Role-based access is foundational for secure service-to-service and human access design.
Identity design should be treated as architecture, not configuration cleanup.
examples: Grant applications scoped access to specific AWS resources using roles.
Control human administration with least-privilege policy boundaries.
pitfalls: Starting with broad permissions and never tightening them.
Treating IAM as isolated from operational and security architecture.
avoid_when: Avoid manual credential sprawl and long-lived broad access patterns.

documents.title: AWS Key Management Service
documents.summary: KMS is the managed control point for encryption keys used across many AWS services.
key_points: KMS reduces the need to build custom key-management infrastructure.
It integrates encryption governance into broader AWS architectures.
It is most useful when teams want encryption controls without operating dedicated key systems.
examples: Control encryption for data stored in supported AWS services.
Apply managed cryptographic governance to application data paths.
pitfalls: Assuming encryption solves access control or data-classification problems by itself.
Treating key management as only a compliance checkbox.
avoid_when: Avoid custom encryption-key sprawl when managed service integration already meets the need.

documents.title: Amazon CloudWatch
documents.summary: CloudWatch is the core AWS observability layer for metrics, logs, alarms, and dashboards.
key_points: CloudWatch provides the baseline operational visibility most AWS systems need.
Metrics and alarms support service health, capacity, and incident response workflows.
It is necessary but not always sufficient for deep distributed diagnostics.
examples: Track API latency, instance health, or queue depth.
Raise alarms on thresholds that indicate failure or saturation.
pitfalls: Collecting telemetry without actionable alarms or dashboard design.
Expecting metrics and logs alone to explain distributed-request behavior.
avoid_when: Avoid treating CloudWatch as the whole observability strategy when tracing or deeper correlation is needed.

documents.title: AWS CloudTrail
documents.summary: CloudTrail records AWS API activity and account events so teams can audit changes and investigate incidents.
key_points: CloudTrail answers who changed what and when.
It is critical for governance, forensics, and compliance workflows.
It complements monitoring by focusing on control-plane activity rather than service health.
examples: Investigate unexpected infrastructure changes or permission modifications.
Support audit evidence for operational and security reviews.
pitfalls: Assuming preventive controls remove the need for audit evidence.
Not planning how logs will be retained and reviewed.
avoid_when: Avoid relying only on dashboards when auditability and investigation are required.

documents.title: AWS X-Ray
documents.summary: X-Ray helps trace requests across distributed systems so teams can understand latency and failure propagation end to end.
key_points: Tracing complements logs and metrics by showing cross-service request paths.
It is especially useful in microservice and serverless architectures.
X-Ray is valuable when end-to-end latency diagnosis matters more than component-local symptoms.
examples: Trace a user request across API, queue, and function boundaries.
Identify which downstream service contributes most to end-to-end latency.
pitfalls: Assuming logs alone can diagnose distributed latency issues.
Adding tracing without a plan for sampling and analysis.
avoid_when: Avoid skipping tracing in architectures where requests cross many service boundaries.
